coredata.dc:title: “Methylene blue-encapsulated superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles Coupled With NIR-responsive upconversion carbon dots as novel nanoplatform for improved photodynamic therapy”

coredata.dc:description: The developing of nanostructured platform suitable for the upconversion photodynamic therapy (UC PDT) is highly desirable to overcome the demerits of the conventional platforms. Herein, this study introduces a novel engineered nanoplatform with tailored surface modifications (PSCM), consists of Upconversion carbon dots (CDs) triggered by near-infrared (NIR) radiation, PEGylated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (PEG-SPIONS), and methylene blue (MB) photosensitizer for enhancing the upconversion PDT. The PEG-SPIONS function as a magnetic nanoscale carrier, offering a biocompatible and chemically stable interface for the co-immobilization of CDs and MB. At this engineered interface, CDs function as optical nanotransducers, converting NIR light into visible emission that activates surface-bound MB to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) within deep tissue environments. The results showed that PSCM with NIR exhibited anticancer efficacy against four cancerous cell lines, including: breast (MCF-7), colorectal (HCT-116), pancreatic (PC3) and liver (HepG-2). In addition, PSCM displayed high potential antiproliferative activity against HepG-2 with IC50 value of 33.65 ng/ml, followed by PC3 with IC50 value of 194.4 ng/ml compared to non-NIR treatment. Moreover, PSCM showed no toxic activity against normal cell lines, including: epithelial (MCF-12F), and fibroblastic (BJ-1). Results from immunoblotting and RT-qPCR assays revealed that treating with PSCM exposed to NIR led to substantial increase in pro-apoptotic protein and mRNA markers for caspase-3, p53, and Bax, while significantly decreasing both protein and mRNA levels of the anti-apoptotic markers Bcl-2. Moreover, PSCM had a controlled ROS production, which was detected by observing the alternation of plasmid DNA (pET-28b) bands position and density on agarose gel electrophoresis. Overall, this nanoplatform offers a pioneering contribution to engineered nanotechnology by demonstrating key physical, chemical, and biological interactions at the nanoscale interface, highlighting how precise surface modification and interfacial engineering can amplify the performance of upconversion-based photodynamic therapy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The developing of nanostructured platform suitable for the upconversion photodynamic therapy (UC PDT) is highly desirable to overcome the demerits of the conventional platforms. Herein, this study introduces a novel engineered nanoplatform with tailored surface modifications (PSCM), consists of Upconversion carbon dots (CDs) triggered by near-infrared (NIR) radiation, PEGylated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (PEG-SPIONS), and methylene blue (MB) photosensitizer for enhancing the upconversion PDT. The PEG-SPIONS function as a magnetic nanoscale carrier, offering a biocompatible and chemically stable interface for the co-immobilization of CDs and MB. At this engineered interface, CDs function as optical nanotransducers, converting NIR light into visible emission that activates surface-bound MB to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) within deep tissue environments. The results showed that PSCM with NIR exhibited anticancer efficacy against four cancerous cell lines, including: breast (MCF-7), colorectal (HCT-116), pancreatic (PC3) and liver (HepG-2). In addition, PSCM displayed high potential antiproliferative activity against HepG-2 with IC50 value of 33.65 ng/ml, followed by PC3 with IC50 value of 194.4 ng/ml compared to non-NIR treatment. Moreover, PSCM showed no toxic activity against normal cell lines, including: epithelial (MCF-12F), and fibroblastic (BJ-1). Results from immunoblotting and RT-qPCR assays revealed that treating with PSCM exposed to NIR led to substantial increase in pro-apoptotic protein and mRNA markers for caspase-3, p53, and Bax, while significantly decreasing both protein and mRNA levels of the anti-apoptotic markers Bcl-2. Moreover, PSCM had a controlled ROS production, which was detected by observing the alternation of plasmid DNA (pET-28b) bands position and density on agarose gel electrophoresis. Overall, this nanoplatform offers a pioneering contribution to engineered nanotechnology by demonstrating key physical, chemical, and biological interactions at the nanoscale interface, highlighting how precise surface modification and interfacial engineering can amplify the performance of upconversion-based photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a highly targeted therapeutic approach for various diseases, including cancer and infectious diseases. It has demonstrated remarkable potential as a non-invasive therapy compared to chemotherapy and radiation [1]. There are three essential components for a successful photodynamic therapy process: surrounding oxygen, a light source with a suitable wavelength, and a light-sensitive molecule called a photosensitizer (PS) [2]. By exposing a PS to a light source with a specific wavelength, the energy is transferred to it, causing it to enter an excited singlet state. The excited singlet state can lose energy through thermal decay, fluorescence emission, or intersystem crossover, transferring energy to a lower excited triplet state [3–5]. Type I and Type II PDT are the two PDT processes that allow the PS to undergo a photochemical reaction with the surrounding molecules in the excited triplet state, producing reactive species. Superoxide anions, hydroxyl radicals, and hydrogen peroxide are examples of reactive free radicals and radical ions that can be produced when a PS in Type I PDT transfers an electron to different receptor biomolecules, such as proteins and nucleic acids. A PS may interact directly with molecular oxygen in Type II PDT, producing large amounts of extremely reactive and cytotoxic oxygen species, which are thought to be the main mechanisms causing target cell death [5–8].

The limited tissue penetration ability of the light utilized to activate PS molecules is a key drawback of photodynamic therapy (PDT) in clinical applications, despite the benefits already highlighted. The majority of PS molecules currently employed in PDT are stimulated by visible or even UV light, which has a relatively shallow penetration depth in biological tissues [9–13]. As a result, PDT cannot be effectively used to treat large or deep-seated cancers.

Consequently, significant efforts have been devoted to the development of novel photodynamic therapy (PDT) techniques that are activated by near-infrared (NIR) light, the wavelengths of which fall within the 'transparency window' of biological tissues. NIR light, with a spectral range of approximately 700–1000 nm, is considered an optimal light source for phototherapies, as it can penetrate tissue to a depth an order of magnitude greater than visible light while exhibiting minimal phototoxicity to healthy cells and tissues [14,15]. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are particles capable of efficiently converting NIR light into visible wavelengths. This conversion activates photosensitizers and generates cytotoxic singlet oxygen, a process of particular significance, as it underscores the potential application of UCNPs in photodynamic therapy (UC PDT) for the treatment of deeply located malignancies. In the monograph Introduction to Biophotonics, Prasad first introduced the concept of Upconversion Photodynamic Therapy (UC PDT), and Zhang et al. were the first to subsequently implement UC PDT using the highly effective NaYF₄:Yb³⁺/Er³⁺ upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [16–17].Studies have demonstrated that the viability of several cancer cell types can be significantly reduced when UCNPs are combined with photosensitizers (PS) and activated by near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation. These UCNP-PS complexes are formed using specific wavelengths; for example, the photosensitizer tris-(bipyridine) ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)₃²⁺) absorbs light maximally at 450 nm, which closely matches the blue emission of Tm³⁺. As a result, singlet oxygen can be generated by synthesizing blue-emitting NaYF₄:Yb³⁺/Tm³⁺ nanoparticles coated with a silica shell and Ru(bpy)₃²⁺ [18]. Additionally, a complex between β-NaYF₄:Yb³⁺/Er³⁺ UCNPs and meso‑tetraphenylporphine photosensitizers resulted in 75 % cell death in HeLa cells [19]. Moreover, a novel nanoplatform combining red-emitting UCNPs with NIR photosensitizers has been developed to enhance cancer treatment. UCNPs functionalized with α-cyclodextrin (α-CD) were used to load three widely employed second-generation photosensitizers—chlorin 6 (Ce6), zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc), and methylene blue (MB)—which showed improved therapeutic outcomes. However, the effectiveness of UC PDT is limited by the relatively low photosensitizer loading efficiency on UCNPs and the luminescence efficiency of the UCNPs themselves [20]. In vivo studies have shown promising results, particularly in a study by Liu et al., who demonstrated the potential of UCNP-based PDT using PEGylated amphiphilic polymer-coated β-NaYF₄:Yb³⁺/Er³⁺ UCNPs noncovalently coated with the photosensitizer Ce6. Upon 980 nm laser excitation, significant tumor shrinkage was observed following direct injection of the UCNP-Ce6 complex into tumor sites [21,22]. Although this study did not demonstrate selective tumor targeting, the use of targeted ligands on UCNPs can enhance the selective accumulation of the UCNP-photosensitizer complex in tumor tissues by recognizing receptors on cancer cells [23,24]. Notably, a tumor-targeting agent-coated mesoporous silica-based UCNP has been developed that facilitates both high photosensitizer loading and selective tumor accumulation [25]. Furthermore, by combining two distinct photosensitizers, which absorb both the green and red emissions of NaYF₄:Yb³⁺/Er³⁺ UCNPs, the therapeutic efficacy of UC PDT has been significantly enhanced compared to single-photosensitizer systems. This dual-photosensitizer approach increases the efficiency of energy use from UCNPs, resulting in superior PDT outcomes [24].

Notwithstanding, the low quantum yield of the upconverting process, insufficient energy transfer between UCNPs and PS molecules, and high quenching of emission in aqueous solution are several of the limitations that have reduced the therapeutic efficacy of UCNPs photodynamic therapy. Moreover, the method of synthesis the UCNPs is complicated and requires detailed systematic methodologies for the synthetic and subsequent surface modification process of rare earth minerals, while other materials, including organic dyes and carbon dots (CDs), may be synthesized with ease [26,27]. Additionally, Rare earth's luminescence is comparatively weaker than that of other nanomaterials, including CDs and organic dyes, when the surface area of the nanomaterials based on rare earth hosts is taken into account. As far as we know, the long-term biosafety of these compounds is yet unclear, hence other essential factors need to be taken into account. In order to lessen the long-term biological cytotoxicity effects of rare earth nanoparticles, further study is necessary in the areas of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics, toxicological parameters, and degradation [27].

In the deep ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared spectrums, carbon dots are defined as discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles (NPs) with a size of <10 nm. Additionally, they exhibit outstanding photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. This means that it can absorb two or more photons in succession under long wavelength radiation such as NIR and convert them into adjustable UV or visible emissions at shorter wavelengths. This ability has generated significant interest in the fields of biosensing [28,29], and bioimaging [30–32]. Because of their distinct photochemical properties, CDs hold promise as nanostructured materials for photo-mediated therapeutic uses. By using hydrothermal or solvothermal techniques, CDs may be readily synthesized from organic molecules to produce a variety of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface [33–36], This results in high solubility and biocompatible CDs without the need for additional surface modification. Therefore, CDs are a promising material for upconversion photodynamic therapy due to their unique photochemical properties.

Xiang et al. developed a complex between porphyrin-based metal-organic frame works (PCN-224) as a nanophotosensitizer and upconversion carbon dots, which transfer the energy to porphyrinic MOFs, for mitochondria-targeted photodynamic therapy. The result is that the capacity to target mitochondria exacerbated ROS oxidative stress, sped up mitochondrial malfunction, and triggered intrinsic apoptosis. The destruction of cancer cells in vitro and the inhibition of tumor development in vivo were excellent demonstrations of anticancer activity [37].

However, this platform lacks cancer cell targeting capability due to the absence of a carrier. Furthermore, PCN-224 exhibits a significantly lower quantum yield (Φ∆∼0.021) compared to several other photosensitizers, including porfimer sodium (Φ∆∼0.89), benzoporphyrin (Φ∆∼0.84), meta-tetra(hydroxyphenyl)chlorin (Φ∆∼0.87), palladium bacteriopheophorbide (Φ∆∼0.5), methylene blue (Φ∆∼0.5), and aluminum phthalocyanine tetrasulphonate (Φ∆∼0.38), all of which demonstrate a higher potential for singlet oxygen generation [27–38-39]. It can be inferred that the potency of this complex is relatively low and limited. There are several solutions that can improve the targetability and potency of any platform.

PEGylated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (PEG-SPIONS), a powerful choice for enhancing platform targetability, are well-known magnetized nanostructured carriers used in various biomedical applications, including drug delivery, photothermal therapy, and photodynamic therapy [40]. Several advantages are associated with the use of PEG-SPIONS in PDT. The polyethylene glycol (PEG) coating on the surface of SPIONS plays a key role in improving the biocompatibility of the nanoplatform and helps increase the loading efficiency of the photosensitizer. In addition to serving as carriers, PEG-SPIONS can circulate in the bloodstream for longer periods by avoiding detection and clearance by immune cells, which ultimately boosts their therapeutic effectiveness. Their surface is also highly adaptable, supporting a wide range of modifications that enhance their overall performance, stability, and potential for precise, targeted biomedical applications. Additionally, the iron oxide core significantly enhances targetability due to its superparamagnetic properties [41–42].

Methylene blue (MB) is a photosensitizer that exhibits intriguing photophysical and photochemical characteristics. With a comparatively high quantum yield (Φ∆∼0.5), it can produce singlet oxygen, and it has shown impressive results in the in vivo regression of cancer and microbial infections [39]. This low-toxicity photosensitizer has a lot of potential for therapeutic PDT applications when it is encapsulated in nanoparticles [43,44]. Therefore, using PEG-SPIONS as a nanocarrier and MB as a potent photosensitizer in a PDT platform is valuable for enhancing targetability and efficacy.

This study aims to introduce an innovative strategy for the more selective and efficient delivery of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to cancer cells, enhancing the precision and effectiveness of the therapeutic response. The engineered nanostructure was developed to serve as a straightforward, tunable, and highly efficient delivery platform, while maintaining a biocompatible and biodegradable profile. In order to synthesize the nanoplatform, polyethylene glycol was functionalized on the surface of superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPIONS) to form the PEGylated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (PEG-SPIONS). Upconversion carbon dots (CDs) have been manufactured and integrated onto the PEG-SPIONS surface, establishing a well-defined nanoscale interface for energy transfer. The resultant nanoparticles underwent self-assembly to complex with methylene blue (MB) as a photosensitizer, thereby enhancing upconversion photodynamic therapy efficiency through controlled interfacial interactions (Scheme 1 ).

Polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW 4000 Da), ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), soluble starch, citric acid, methylene blue (MB), and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) solution were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize Upconversion carbon dots. In this process, 2 g of soluble starch and 1 g of citric acid were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated to 150 °C for five hours, resulting in a reddish-brown solution. To remove larger particles, the solution was mixed with an equal volume of ethanol and centrifuged at 16,000 rpm for 10 min. The soluble fraction was further purified by dialysis in distilled water for 48 hours using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 300 Da [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEG coated iron oxide nanoparticles (PEG-SPIONS)
ce:section.ce:para: Fe₃O₄ nanoparticles were synthesized using the co-precipitation method. First, 2 g of PEG was completely dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water at 40 °C under continuous stirring. A 1:2 molar ratio of 1.39 g of FeSO₄·7H₂O and 2.71 g of FeCl₃·6H₂O was added to the solution. After the PEG and iron salts were fully dissolved, 10 mL of a 0.75 M NH₄OH solution was added dropwise at a rate of 0.6 mL/min with vigorous stirring. The suspension was stirred for an additional 30 min at 40 °C following the addition of NH₄OH. The resulting ferrofluid was placed on a permanent magnet to facilitate the separation of magnetic particles. The supernatant was decanted, and the ferrofluid was washed multiple times with ethanol to remove excess polymer and reaction residues. This washing step also addressed reversible flocculation and rapid sedimentation observed before the washing process [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEG coated iron oxide with carbon dots complex (PSC)
ce:section.ce:para: The PEG-coated Fe₃O₄ nanoparticles (NPs) were added to the carbon dots (CDs) solution and stirred continuously at 40 °C for 24 hours. The resulting product was separated using a super-magnet and vacuum-dried at 70 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEG coated iron oxide with carbon dots and methylene blue complex (PSCM)
ce:section.ce:para: The PSC complex was immersed in a methylene blue (MB) solution and stirred continuously at 40 °C for 24 hours. The product was then collected using a super-magnet and vacuum-dried at 70 °C.

The nanoparticles were characterized using several analytical techniques. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu 8400 s, Japan) was used to detect functional groups, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Shimadzu X-Lab 600, Japan) was employed to determine crystalline phases at 40 kV using Cu Kα radiation. The chemical composition was detected by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific – US). The shape and morphology of the synthesized nanocarriers were visualized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL 2100 microscope) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Magnetic properties were evaluated with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Lakeshore Model 7410). The zeta potential of the samples was measured using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK). Optical properties of the nanocomplex were analyzed using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer and a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer.

The efficiency of methylene blue (MB) loading into the nanocarrier was determined using the UV–Vis spectrophotometric method. The complex was ultracentrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 30 min, and the absorbance of the free MB in the supernatant was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The concentration of unattached MB was calculated using Beer’s law and a calibration curve of MB absorbance at 665 nm [48,49].

The entrapment efficiency (EE%) of MB within the nanoparticles was calculated using the following equation [49]:

The release of methylene blue (MB) from the PSCM complex was studied using a two-compartment diffusion cell system. A cellulose membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 12,000 Da was used to separate the compartments. The total amount of MB loaded into the nanocarrier was equivalent to 30 mg. In the donor compartment, the PSCM complex was dissolved in 1 mL of distilled water, while the receiver compartment contained 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The MB concentration in the receiver compartment was measured at specific time intervals: 15 min, 30 min, 1 hour, 2 hours, 4 hours, 24 hours, 48 hours, 72 hours, 96 hours, and 120 hours. This process continued daily until 100 % of the loaded MB was released or no significant further release was observed. The released MB concentration was quantified using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at the maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) of 665 nm. Each measurement was performed in triplicate to ensure accuracy. To analyze the release kinetics of MB from the nanoplatform, various mathematical models were applied using OriginPro software. These models included zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer–Peppas, and Hixson–Crowell models, providing insights into the release mechanism [50].

To evaluate cytotoxicity, cell viability was assessed using the MTT assay (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) in 96-well culture plates [51]. The study included four cancer cell lines—MCF-7 (breast adenocarcinoma), HCT-116 (colon carcinoma), PC3 (human pancreatic carcinoma), and HepG-2 (liver carcinoma)—along with two non-cancerous cell lines, MCF-12F (human mammary epithelial cells) and BJ-1 (human skin fibroblasts) from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). Cancer cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic solution (10,000 µg/mL streptomycin sulfate, 25 µg/mL amphotericin B, 10,000 U/mL potassium penicillin, and 1 % l-glutamine; Biowest, Riverside, MO, USA). BJ-1 fibroblasts were maintained in complete DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium). All cell cultures were conducted under aseptic conditions and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO₂. Cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 10⁴ cells per well and incubated for 24 hours to allow adherence. The medium was then replaced with fresh medium containing the PSCM nanocomplex or MB at varying concentrations (3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 250 ng/mL). Directly after treatment, the cells were either kept in the dark or exposed to an 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser source (0.5 W/cm²) for 20 min, after which they were incubated for 48 hours. After the incubation period, 40 µL of MTT solution (2.5 µg/mL; Bio Basic Canada Inc., Toronto, ON, Canada) was added to each well, and the plates were incubated for an additional 4 hours. The reaction was halted by adding 200 µL of 10 % sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) solution, and the plates were incubated overnight at 37 °C to dissolve the formazan crystals formed by metabolically active cells. The absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at 595 nm with a reference wavelength of 690 nm using a microplate reader (model 3350, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). Cytotoxicity was quantified using the following formula:

This experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure accuracy and reproducibility of the results. The concentration required to induce 50 % cell inhibition (IC₅₀) was determined by treating the cells with varying concentrations of the PSCM nanocomplex. The data were analyzed using probit analysis, and statistical significance was assessed with a t-test (SPSS version 11.0, Chicago, IL, USA).

The expression levels of pro-apoptotic and anti-apoptotic markers were analyzed in HepG-2 and PC3 cells treated with the IC₅₀ concentration of PSCM and exposed to a 20-minute, of 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser for 48 hours. Following treatment, the cells were harvested, and total RNA was isolated using the Total RNA Purification Kit (Norgen Biotek Corp., Thorold, ON, Canada). Purified RNA was then used to synthesize cDNA (1 µg) in a final volume of 20 µL using the QuantiTect Reverse Transcriptase Kit (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany). Thermal cycling was performed on a Rotor-Gene Q 5-Plex HRM thermal cycler (Qiagen, Germany), and the specificity of PCR amplicons was verified using the QuantiTect SYBR-Green PCR Kit (Qiagen, Germany) following standard protocols [52]. The primer sequences used were as follows:
•
Bax: 5′-AAGCTGAGCGAGTGTCTCCGGCG-3′ (forward) and 5′-CAGATGCCGGTTCAGGTACTCAGTC-3′ (reverse)
•
B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2): 5′-CTCGTCGCTACCGTCGTGACTTGG-3′ (forward) and 5′-CAGATGCCGGTTCAGGTACTCAGTC-3′ (reverse)
•
p53: 5′-GCTCTGACTGTACCACCATCC-3′ (forward) and 5′-CTCTCGGAACATCTCGAAGCG-3′ (reverse)
•
Caspase-3: 5′-CAAACTTTTTCAGAGGGGATCG-3′ (forward) and 5′-GCATACTGTTTCAGCATGGCA-3′ (reverse)
•
VEGFR1: 5′-GCACATGACGGAAGGAAGAC-3′ (forward) and 5′-TTCGCAGTTCAGCAGTCCTA-3′ (reverse)
•
Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH): 5′-CGGAGTCAACGGATTTGGTC-3′ (forward) and 5′-AGCCTTCTCCATGGTCGTGA-3′ (reverse) (internal control)

Each reaction was carried out in a final volume of 25 µL, comprising 5 µL of diluted cDNA template, 12.5 µL of 2× SYBR-Green PCR Master Mix, 2.5 µL of each primer (10 µM stock), and 2.5 µL of RNase-free water. Triplicate technical replicates were performed for each biological sample, with a no-template control included to ensure specificity. Raw data were analyzed using Rotor-Gene® 2.1 software (Qiagen GmbH, Düsseldorf, Germany) to determine the threshold cycle (Ct) using the second derivative maximum. After normalization to GAPDH expression, fold-change values for each gene were calculated using the 2⁻ΔΔCt method.

Western blotting was carried out in accordance with earlier instructions [53–54]. HepG-2 and PC3 cells were gathered and homogenized in protein extraction buffer (Roche) following 48 hours of exposure to the PSCM IC50 concentration and NIR treatment. Two centrifugations of the extracts were performed at 13,000× g for 10 min at 4 °C. The Bradford assay (Bio-Rad) was used to measure the protein contents in the supernatants after they were collected. For every sample, 2× loading buffer was combined with 20 µg of protein extract, heated to 95 °C, and separated on 12 % SDS-PAGE gels. Once transferred to a polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane, the membrane was blocked using a solution of 5 % nonfat milk diluted with 0.1 % Tween-20 in phosphate-buffered saline. The primary and secondary antibodies were diluted 1:2000 in 1 % defatted milk diluted with 0.1 % Tween-20. Anti-Bax E63 monoclonal (ab32503), anti-Bcl-2 monoclonal E17 (ab32124), anti-Caspase-3 polyclonal (ab13847), anti-β-actin monoclonal SP124 (ab115777), and anti-p53 monoclonal PAb 240 (ab26) produced by Abcam (Cambridge, UK) were the main antibodies used to detect target proteins. Additionally, there were secondary antibodies against rabbit IgG (GE Healthcare, Milwaukee, WI, USA) and anti-mouse IgG (Amersham Biosciences, Buckinghamshire, UK). The β-actin detected band was used as loading control for normalization.

This study, which detects ROS generation and evaluates potential DNA damage activity by exposing the photosensitizer to an appropriate wavelength, is based on a previous study reported by [55]. The ROS generation was assessed using two different concentrations of PSCM: 0.5 µg/mL and 1.25 µg/mL. Each concentration was separately added to 5 µL of pET-28b plasmid DNA (60 µg/µL), and the final volume was adjusted to 10 µL with phosphate buffer (8.3 mM, pH 7.4). The samples were then treated with 20 min of NIR radiation or left untreated. The final mixture was incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. Additionally, 5 µL of pET-28b plasmid DNA (25 µg/µL) was used as a control for DNA damage detection. Furthermore, similar procedures were performed with 5 µL of methylene blue (MB) at 0.5 µg/mL, both with and without NIR treatment, and with 5 µL of Fenton’s reagent. The composition of this reagent is a mixture of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) and ferrous sulfate salt (FeSO₄) [56]. After 20 min, the samples were loaded into a 1.5 % agarose gel, and the separated bands were analyzed.

The FT-IR spectra for the four stages of the nanosystem synthesis were investigated separately (Fig. 1 ). There is a remarkable similarity between the IR spectra of PEG-SPIONS, PSC, and PSCM materials. All of them exhibit the characteristic bands of Fe-O vibrations in the spinel structure's tetrahedral sites at approximately 630 cm⁻¹ and 570 cm⁻¹, indicating the presence of Fe₃O₄ [46,57]. Additionally, the CO stretching band at 1050 cm⁻¹ and the CH stretching band at approximately 2920 cm⁻¹ correspond to PEG molecules on the surface of the nanostructured carriers [58]. The wavenumber at 883 cm⁻¹ is assigned to CH bending out-of-plane in aromatic compounds and NH bending, due to the presence of the methylene blue molecule in the PSCM sample [59]. All the molecules exhibit similar bands at approximately 3435 cm⁻¹, 1631 cm⁻¹, and 1380 cm⁻¹. The band at 3435 cm⁻¹ corresponds to OH stretching, while the band at 1631 cm⁻¹ is attributed to C = C stretching. The band at 1380 cm⁻¹ is assigned to -OH bending and CO-H in-plane bending vibrations, indicating the presence of PEG molecules and carbon dots (CDs) [45,57]. Moreover, the presence of methylene blue is confirmed by the CN stretching vibration of aromatic amines at 1380 cm⁻¹ [59]. In conclusion, the FT-IR analysis validates the incorporation of methylene blue, carbon dots, polyethylene glycol, and iron oxide in the synthesized complex.

The crystallographic examination of CDs, PEG-SPIONS, PSC, and PSCM was conducted using the powder XRD technique to examine their surface modifications and structural integrity. The X-ray diffraction patterns of PEG-SPIONS (Fig. 2 ) reveal that all the diffraction peak positions and relative intensities match those of magnetite Fe₃O₄, corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes as listed in the JCPDS card (19–029) at approximately 2θ = 30.17°, 35.9°, 43.38°, 53.39°, 57.50°, and 62.90°, respectively [36]. This confirms that the iron oxide nanoparticles are pure and exhibit a cubic spinel structure [46,47,60]. Additionally, the surface modification of Fe₃O₄ with polyethylene glycol was confirmed by the appearance of minor peaks at 2θ = 23° [46,60], highlighting the interfacial interactions between the nanoparticle surface and PEG. The XRD pattern of CDs showed a broad peak at a 2θ value of 21°, indicative of weakly crystalline graphitic carbon [45]. However, the background of the PSC and PSCM samples displays a hump at low angles (below 21°), attributed to the presence of carbon dots in the systems. As shown in Fig. 2, the XRD pattern of PSCM displays a significant increase in crystallinity, which can be attributed to the incorporation of methylene blue (MB) onto the PSC surface, leading to a more ordered structure.

The addition of methylene blue (MB) to PSC alters the atomic structure and composition of the resultant PSCM complex. This alteration, detected by the XPS survey, is shown in Fig. 3 . Notably, the atomic percentage of nitrogen (N1s) increases in the PSCM complex compared to PSC. Additionally, two new atomic peaks emerge: sulfur (S2p) and chlorine (Cl2p). These findings confirm the surface interactions between PSC and MB. The sulfur originates from the sulfonate group present in the methylene blue molecule, chlorine derives from residuals during methylene blue manufacturing, and nitrogen contributes significantly to the surface composition of MB [61]. To further explore these changes, high-resolution XPS spectra of PSC and PSCM were analyzed, as shown in Figure S1. Significant differences between the two complexes are observed in Fe2p, O1s, N1s, C1s, S2p, and Cl2p spectra. The Fe2p spectrum of PSC exhibits peaks at 711.29 eV with a 22.19 % atomic percentage and 713.85 eV with a 22.16 % atomic percentage, corresponding to the oxidation states of Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺. In PSCM, the Fe2p peak slightly shifts to 711.12 eV (24.48 %), indicating minimal alterations in the iron electronic structure due to MB adsorption. Additionally, PSCM exhibits a stronger peak at 713.48 eV (19.97 %), indicating increased surface interactions between iron and methylene blue [62–64]. The O1s spectra reveal variations in the oxygen environment. In PSC, the primary peak at 532.23 eV (44.33 %) represents oxygen in hydroxyl groups or PEG functionalities, while the peak at 530.26 eV (35.85 %) corresponds to metal-oxygen bonds in the SPION core. In PSCM, MB adsorption causes the main O1s peak to shift to 530.46 eV (57.35 %), indicating an increase in metal-oxygen interactions. The peak at 533.55 eV (6.07 %), associated with adsorbed water or C = O groups, diminishes, suggesting these species are replaced by methylene blue [64,65]. The N1s spectra show a shift from 401.79 eV (100 %) in PSC to 400.69 eV (100 %) in PSCM due to the nitrogen introduced by methylene blue [61]. In the C1s spectra of PSC, peaks are observed for shake-up satellites at 291.33 eV (16.51 %), carboxyl or carbonyl groups at 288.59 eV (35.83 %), and CC/CH bonds at 285.88 eV (47.66 %). Upon MB adsorption, the C1s peak shifts to 285.53 eV (61.67 %), indicating an increase in aliphatic CC/CH bonds. Additional peaks at 288.75 eV (25.67 %) and 286.93 eV (12.66 %) suggest interactions between functionalized carbon species and nitrogen-containing groups in MB [63,64]. The presence of sulfur and chlorine in PSCM, absent in PSC, is confirmed by the S2p and Cl2p peaks. The Cl2p peaks at 198.81 eV (68.82 %) and 203.44 eV (31.18 %) correspond to Cl⁻ ions, while the S2p peaks at 165.12 eV (58.87 %) and 171.53 eV (41.13 %) indicate sulfonate groups [61]. These results clearly show that methylene blue interacts with PSC, leading to the formation of the PSCM complex and causing significant changes to its surface chemistry and interfacial properties.

The zeta potentials of CDs, PEG-SPIONS, PSC, and PSCM are measured as −23 mV, −24 mV, −19 mV, and −20 mV, respectively, showing no significant change in the surface charge among the four molecules. All the molecules exhibit a similar negative potential, indicating good stability of the different moieties. The stable negative surface charge indicates that electrostatic repulsion is effectively preventing aggregation, keeping the particles evenly dispersed in solution. This stability is essential for maintaining the biocompatibility and ensuring reliable performance of the nanoplatforms, especially in biomedical applications [58].

The M-H hysteresis loops of PEG-SPIONS, PSC, and PSCM nanoparticles were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) up to 15 kG. As shown in Fig. 6 , all magnetization curves exhibit an S-like shape under the influence of an external magnetic field. These materials are confirmed to be superparamagnetic, as their M-H plots display zero coercivity [60]. When the external magnetic field is removed, no residual magnetism is observed, further confirming the zero coercivity characteristic [66]. The saturation magnetization (Ms) values for PEG-SPIONS, PSC, and PSCM are 46.759, 42.664, and 46.296 emu g⁻¹, respectively.

The upconversion carbon dots (CDs) exhibit a sharp absorbance peak at 280 nm (Fig. 7 ). This was ascribed to n-π* transition, consistent with previous literature [45]. PEG-SPIONS display a broad absorbance band ranging from 200 nm to 600 nm [58]. Consequently, the absorption spectrum of PSC combines the sharp peak of CDs with the broad spectrum of PEG-SPIONS. Finally, the PSCM spectrum includes the characteristic peaks of the methylene blue molecule at 250 nm, 632 nm, and 665 nm, along with the characteristic peaks of CDs and PEG-SPIONS [67]. This indicates that the PSCM complex is composed of all the molecules in a favorable balance.

The emission spectrum of PSCM was analyzed at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 850 nm to 900 nm, covering the NIR region (Fig. 8a ). The results show that PSCM produces the same emission spectrum regardless of the excitation wavelength, with a major peak at 693 nm, indicating the fluorescence emission of methylene blue [67,68]. The highest emission intensity occurs at an excitation wavelength of 860 nm. In contrast, neither methylene blue (MB) nor PEG-SPIONS alone exhibit emission when excited in the NIR region. This confirms that the upconversion carbon dots (CDs) are responsible for absorbing wavelengths in the NIR range (850–900 nm). This absorption triggers a multiphoton activation mechanism, resulting in the emission of light at wavelengths between 500 nm and 640 nm with varying intensities (Fig. 8b) [45]. The emitted wavelengths from the CDs serve as the excitation source for methylene blue, producing its characteristic emission spectrum with a maximum peak at 693 nm, as shown in Fig. 8a [67]. Moreover, these processes can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), including singlet oxygen, as indicated by the photoluminescence spectrum of methylene blue within the nanoplatform [45].

The entrapment efficiency (EE%) of methylene blue (MB) in the PSCM nanoplatform was calculated using the MB calibration curve and was determined to be approximately 72 % (Figure S3). This high EE% indicates a significant attachment of the MB molecule to the nanoplatform.

As shown in Figure S4, the cumulative release percentage of methylene blue from PSCM reached 98 % after five days. The formulation demonstrated a significant delay in MB release, with the maximum amount released after 24 hours. Several kinetic models, including zero order, first order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, and Hixson-Crowell, were fitted to the release profile of methylene blue from the nanoplatform. The fitted equations are summarized in Table 1 . The Korsmeyer–Peppas model, with the highest R², was the most appropriate model for the methylene blue release profile. The release process is represented by the diffusional exponent n in the Korsmeyer–Peppas model, which describes drug release from a polymeric structure. The value of the diffusional exponent for the particle release profile represents Fick diffusion for values up to 0.5 and non-Fickian diffusion for values between 0.5 and 1.0, or > 1.0 (0.5 〈 n < 1 for anomalous transport, n = 1 for Case-II transport, and n 〉 1 for Super Case-II transport) [50]. Since n was smaller than 0.5, the PSCM formulation followed a Fick diffusion model.

The antiproliferative effects of PSCM treatment, with or without NIR radiation, against the cell lines are shown in Fig. 9a and 9b, respectively. Growth inhibition in the cancer cell lines MCF-7, PC3, HepG-2, and HCT-116 increased with higher concentrations of PSCM at a constant NIR dose (20 min), as illustrated in Fig. 9a. All tested cancer cell lines exhibited significant growth inhibition in response to PSCM with NIR treatment, with HepG-2 and PC3 showing the highest inhibition. In contrast, treatment with PSCM without NIR radiation had no anticancer effect (Fig. 9b). Cells treated with PSCM and NIR radiation displayed significantly greater antiproliferative effects compared to those treated with PSCM alone. No significant effects were observed in noncancerous MCF-12F or BJ-1 cells with either PSCM treatment. The IC50 values, calculated for PSCM with or without NIR radiation, are shown in Table 2 . Cells treated with PSCM/+NIR exhibited lower IC50 values against cancer cell lines, with HepG-2 showing the lowest IC50 (33.65 ng/ml), followed by PC3 (196.4 ng/ml) and MCF-7 (232.2 ng/ml). In contrast, PSCM/-NIR treatment required higher concentrations, indicating lower antiproliferative effectiveness. Both PSCM/+NIR and PSCM/-NIR had similar effects on noncancerous cells, with nearly unchanged IC50 values. These results suggest that PSCM combined with NIR treatment selectively inhibits cancer cells. Fig. 9c and 9d illustrate the growth inhibition effects of MB with or without NIR radiation against MCF-7, HepG-2, PC3, HCT-116, MCF-12F, and BJ-1, while Table 2 shows the corresponding IC50 values. NIR treatment did not significantly affect MB efficacy against cell lines. However, MB/+NIR exhibited lower IC50 values than MB/-NIR across cell lines, though the difference was not statistically significant. PSCM showed much lower IC50 values than MB in tumor cells when combined with NIR, indicating greater potency against cancer cell lines. Interestingly, MB with NIR showed lower IC50 values than PSCM/+NIR in noncancerous cell lines, further highlighting the selective activity of PSCM against tumor cells. The size and surface properties of the PSCM complex play a crucial role in its ability to preferentially penetrate tumor cells over normal cells. The nanoscale size of PSCM enhances tumor accumulation through the Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect [69], while the PEG coating improves biocompatibility and minimizes nonspecific interactions with normal cells [41]. Additionally, tumor cells are more susceptible to reactive oxygen species (ROS), while normal cells possess more efficient antioxidant defenses [70]. Thus, PSCM/+NIR represents as a potent therapeutic agent in targeted cancer treatment, particularly HepG-2 cell line.

PSCM showed significant efficacy against HepG-2 and PC3 cancer cell lines, as indicated by the IC50 values. To investigate the mechanism underlying the growth-inhibitory effects of PSCM, the expression levels of key apoptotic genes were analyzed in HepG-2 and PC3 cells following treatment with the IC50 concentration of PSCM/+NIR, as shown in Fig. 10 . Real-time PCR results revealed a significant upregulation of pro-apoptotic genes, including p53, Bax, and caspase-3, and a downregulation of the anti-apoptotic genes Bcl-2 and VEGFR1 in both cancer cell lines. The results were compared to those induced by doxorubicin: a well-documented chemotherapeutic agent known for its cytotoxic effects. Notably, PSCM and doxorubicin exhibited comparable efficacy against the HepG-2 cell line. In contrast, doxorubicin showed a significantly higher effect compared to PSCM in targeting the PC3 cell line. These alterations in gene expression suggest that PSCM-mediated photodynamic therapy (PDT) effectively induces apoptosis in HepG-2 and PC3 cancer cells by enhancing pro-apoptotic signals and downregulating anti-apoptotic signals. Previous studies have investigated the role of methylene blue (MB) activation via a 660 nm diode laser on the expression of key apoptotic genes in the human HNSCC cell line (HN5, NCBI, C196). The results of these studies indicated significant changes in gene expression following treatment, specifically the downregulation of the Bcl-2 gene (an anti-apoptotic factor) and the upregulation of the Bax gene (a pro-apoptotic factor) [71].

Immunoblotting against p53, caspase-3, Bax, and Bcl-2 was employed to trace the course of the cell death program in HepG-2 and PC3 cell lines, as they were significantly affected by PSCM. The cells were incubated for 72 hours with the respective treatments to investigate potential processes in more detail. As shown in Fig. 11 , the results revealed that, compared to the non-exposed cells, the levels of p53, caspase-3, and Bax proteins were elevated in both cell lines treated with IC50 concentrations of PSCM and doxorubicin (Dox). Notably, PSCM-treated PC3 cells exhibited higher levels of p53, caspase-3, and Bax compared to Dox-treated PC3 cells. The HepG-2 cell line, on the other hand, showed similar protein levels in both PSCM and Dox treatments. Comparing the treated groups (PSCM and doxorubicin) to the untreated controls showed a decrease in the levels of the anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 protein. These findings are consistent with the activation of apoptotic pathways, contributing to the reduction in cell viability observed in the treated cancer cell lines. This molecular evidence supports the conclusion that PSCM treatments effectively induce apoptosis in cancer cells, enhancing their therapeutic potential. Consistent with previously reported data, exposure of THP-1 macrophages to 10 μM methylene blue (MB), followed by 40 J/cm² laser irradiation, activated reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the mitochondrial-dependent apoptotic pathway, as demonstrated by Western blot analysis. This study confirmed that MB-PDT (photodynamic therapy) induced apoptosis in macrophages primarily through these mechanisms. However, the apoptotic response was significantly reduced when cells were treated with the pan-caspase inhibitor Z-VAD-FMK or the exogenous antioxidant glutathione (GSH) [72].

This experiment hypothesizes that PSCM can generate ROS, including singlet oxygen, which leads to single- and double-strand breaks in plasmid DNA, thereby altering band position and density on agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. 12 ) [73]. In this study, PSCM, when exposed to NIR radiation at two different concentrations (0.5 and 1.25 µg/ml), was compared with methylene blue and Fenton’s reagent. Fenton’s reagent, a source of highly reactive hydroxyl radicals, plays a crucial role in various oxidative processes. Based on the results shown in Fig. 12, it can be concluded that PSCM exposed to NIR radiation exhibited effects comparable to those of Fenton’s reagent, causing total damage to the pET-28b DNA bands [74]. Figure S5 further illustrates the differences in band area for each sample compared to the control (untreated plasmid DNA, 100 %). Treatment with both concentrations of PSCM exposed to NIR showed the absence of supercoiled and circular forms and the appearance of faint linear DNA bands, indicating severe DNA damage [75]. In contrast, the band area of supercoiled and circular forms of DNA treated with PSCM (0.5 µg/ml) without NIR radiation (85 % circular, 0 % linear, 92 % supercoiled) and PSCM (1.25 µg/ml) without NIR radiation (85 % circular, 0 % linear, 81.4 % supercoiled) did not significantly differ from the control (100 % circular, 0 % linear, 100 % supercoiled). Furthermore, the pET-28b bands treated with methylene blue, whether irradiated with NIR or not, showed band areas comparable to the control, with no significant differences. Additionally, no significant reduction in band density was observed in the sample containing DNA exposed to NIR radiation alone. These results confirm that PSCM functions as a systematic platform to generate ROS, as neither methylene blue nor NIR radiation alone produced ROS without the complete platform. The fragmentation of DNA band density after NIR exposure suggests that PSCM has potent photodynamic activity driven by ROS generation. This disruption in DNA band density supports the theory that NIR-induced ROS production can cause severe oxidative damage to cellular components, similar to the effect of Fenton’s reagent. These findings suggest that NIR-mediated therapy could be an effective strategy for inducing oxidative DNA damage in targeted cells while minimizing the impact on non-exposed cells. This mechanism highlights the therapeutic potential of PSCM in selective cancer treatment, where controlled ROS production specifically targets cancerous cells while preserving healthy tissues. Further research to optimize dosage and exposure time may enhance the therapeutic efficacy of this approach.

In conclusion, this research introduces a novel method to overcome the limitations of previous platforms used in upconversion photodynamic therapy (PDT), offering significant improvements for PDT applications. This is achieved through the development of a flexible nanostructured platform called PSCM, which consists of PEGylated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanostructured carrier, the photosensitizer methylene blue, and NIR-light activated upconversion carbon dots. CDs can convert NIR light into visible wavelengths, which are used to excite the methylene blue anchored on the surface of the PEG-SPIONS. This excitation enables methylene blue to interact with the surrounding oxygen molecules, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). These ROS are responsible for inducing cellular damage and selectively killing cancerous cells through oxidative stress. This addresses one of the main challenges of conventional PDT, which requires visible light and cannot penetrate deep tissues. The PSCM nanoplatform exhibited remarkable anticancer activity against various cancer cell lines, including breast, colon, pancreas, and liver. Notably, it demonstrated a strong anti-proliferative effect on liver cancer cells with an IC50 value as low as 33.65 ng/mL, while remaining non-toxic to normal cells. Mechanistic studies suggested that exposure to NIR light significantly decreased anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 levels while increasing pro-apoptotic markers such as Bax, p53, and caspase-3, indicating that the PSCM system effectively induces apoptosis in cancer cells. Moreover, PSCM treated with NIR radiation generated reactive oxygen species (ROS), including singlet oxygen, comparable to Fenton’s reagent, which led to single- and double-strand breaks in plasmid DNA and altered the position and density of DNA bands on electrophoresis. Upconversion PDT may be an effective strategy for inducing oxidative DNA damage in targeted cells. By controlling ROS production, malignant cells can be preferentially targeted while healthy tissues are preserved, highlighting the therapeutic potential of PSCM in selective cancer treatment. Overall, this study represents a significant advancement in upconversion PDT by harmonizing the surface interactions between the components of the PSCM complex, demonstrating enhancements in targetability, biocompatibility, and potency compared to previously used platforms.
Upon request, the corresponding author will provide the data that support the study's conclusions. Privacy and ethical issues prevent the public from accessing the data.
